ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for white fungus and longan sweet soup. Mapped to broader soup food product class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for white fungus longan soup. A0CKM (sweet soups and porridges) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category; used as closest_parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Chinese dessert soup. SNOMED CT coverage of regional Asian dessert foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of white fungus and longan soup is approximately 250 g, typically served warm or chilled as a dessert or snack.

nutrient_source.title: White Fungus and Longan Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for White Fungus and Longan Soup (cr_id F02050024). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this item; set to 0.0 as fat is 0.5 g/100g and the food is plant-based with negligible saturated fat. Detailed fatty acid breakdown, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB for this item; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of sucrose and sugar-sweetened aqueous preparations — analogous estimate based on sucrose GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for white fungus and longan soup exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate source being rock sugar (sucrose, GI ~65 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by aqueous dilution and white fungus beta-glucan polysaccharides providing minor viscosity. The effective GI is estimated at 55, which falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (GI ≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (55 × 32.0 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 17.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.7 g/100g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.5 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite very low sodium (9 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g and request reduced or no added rock sugar, as the high sugar load promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and offsets the benefit of the naturally low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 11.7 g/100g with only 0.5 g/100g dietary fibre and a GI of 55 produces a high glycemic load per serving (GL ~17.6 for 250 g), with minimal protein or fat to buffer postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dessert soup due to its high sugar content and high glycemic load per serving; if consumed, portion should not exceed 100 g and rock sugar should be eliminated or replaced with a low-GI sweetener.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low sodium (9 mg/100g), negligible saturated fat, and low total fat (0.5 g/100g) are cardiovascular-neutral; however, high added sugar (11.7 g/100g) is a concern for triglyceride elevation with regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this soup is low in sodium and saturated fat, but the high added sugar content (11.7 g/100g) should be moderated to avoid adverse effects on serum triglycerides; consume occasionally in small portions with reduced rock sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White Fungus and Longan Soup (雪耳龙眼糖水, Xuě ěr lóngyǎn táng shuǐ) is a traditional Chinese sweet dessert soup widely consumed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia. It is prepared by simmering dried white fungus (Tremella fuciformis, also called snow fungus or snow ear) together with dried or fresh longan fruit in water, sweetened with rock sugar. The resulting soup has a delicate, lightly sweet flavour with a subtle floral note from the longan. The white fungus contributes a soft, gelatinous, slightly crunchy texture and is prized in traditional Chinese medicine for its moistening and lung-nourishing properties, attributed to its beta-glucan polysaccharide content. The soup is served warm in cooler months and chilled in hot weather, and is a common household dessert, festive offering, and snack in Singapore, Malaysia, Hong Kong, and Taiwan. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (56 kcal/100g) and very low in sodium (9 mg/100g), but contains significant added sugar (11.7 g/100g from rock sugar) with minimal dietary fibre (0.5 g/100g), protein (0.2 g/100g), and fat (0.5 g/100g). The high sugar content relative to fibre results in a low-boundary glycemic index (~55) and a high glycemic load per standard 250 g serving (~17.6), making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The white fungus polysaccharides have documented immunomodulatory and antioxidant properties in vitro, but these benefits are substantially offset by the high added sugar content in the traditional preparation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02050024) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match to the canonical Singapore HPB record for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database sucrose reference, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat is set to 0.0 g/100g: total fat is only 0.5 g/100g and the food is entirely plant-based (white fungus, longan, rock sugar, water); saturated fat is negligible and confirmed near-zero by ingredient composition. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this traditional dessert soup are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F02050024) or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for white fungus and longan soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. The estimate is based on sucrose (rock sugar) as the dominant carbohydrate source (GI ~65 per University of Sydney) moderated by aqueous dilution and white fungus polysaccharides. GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low GI category (GI ≤55); gi_category is set to 'low' accordingly. 4. FoodEx2 code A0CKM (sweet soups and porridges) is used as the closest defensible parent category; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific dish; foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent'. 5. FoodOn URI mapped to broader soup food product class; no species-specific FoodOn term for Tremella-longan sweet soup exists. 6. Glycemic load per serving: (55 × 32.0 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 17.6.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values ['exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values ['exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'] — this was the g5 hard gate failure identified by the validator.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low GI category (GI ≤55 = low; GI 56–69 = medium; GI ≥70 = high), resolving the q4 failure identified by the validator.
Iteration 3: Updated glycemic_load_per_serving from 17.7 to 17.6 for arithmetic precision: (55 × 32.0) / 100 = 17.6.
Iteration 3: Updated gi_source notes and notes field to reflect corrected gi_category 'low' and precise GL calculation.
